attributes.stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to the labour ward at 39 weeks gestation in established labour. This is her first pregnancy which has been uncomplicated. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. The cervix is 6 cm dilated. Vaginal examination reveals a thin, pale, longitudinal septum extending from the posterior vaginal wall. The fetal head is engaged with vertex presentation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management of the vaginal septum?
options: Ligation and separation with local anaesthetic
Ligation and separation with general anaesthesia
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a longitudinal vaginal septum discovered during labour. Vaginal septa are congenital anomalies that result from incomplete fusion or resorption of the müllerian ducts during embryological development. While often asymptomatic, they can cause obstruction during labour.

Ligation and separation with local anaesthetic is the most appropriate management in this scenario. The septum should be clamped at its base, ligated to prevent bleeding, and divided. This allows the vaginal canal to accommodate delivery while minimising the risk of bleeding or trauma. Local anaesthetic is sufficient for this procedure and avoids unnecessary risks of general anaesthesia in labour.

Expectant management is inappropriate as the septum may tear during delivery, leading to uncontrolled bleeding. Additionally, it may obstruct the descent of the fetal head.

Immediate caesarean section is not necessary as a thin vaginal septum can be safely managed to allow vaginal delivery. Caesarean section would expose the mother to unnecessary surgical risks.

Deferring management until after delivery could result in traumatic tearing of the septum during delivery with associated bleeding and complications.

Ligation and separation with general anaesthesia would be excessive for this procedure. General anaesthesia carries unnecessary risks during labour and the procedure can be performed adequately under local anaesthetic.

After delivery, the patient should be referred for investigation of other potential müllerian anomalies, as vaginal septa are often associated with other reproductive tract abnormalities.
learningPoints: A vaginal septum discovered in labour should be managed by ligation and separation to prevent bleeding and allow delivery.
Vaginal septa are often asymptomatic until pregnancy and labour.
Longitudinal vaginal septa are associated with other müllerian anomalies and should prompt investigation after delivery.
references: Akhtar MA, Saravelos SH, Li TC, Jayaprakasan K; on behalf of the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Reproductive implications and management of congenital uterine anomalies. Scientific Impact Paper No. 62. BJOG. 2019 Nov 20; DOI: 10.1111/1471-0528.15968.
Blanton EN, Rouse DJ. Trial of labor in women with transverse vaginal septa. Obstet Gynecol. 2003 May;101(5 Pt 2):1110-2. doi: 10.1016/s0029-7844(02)02623-6. PMID: 12738119.
